coredata.dc:title: Macrophage-biomimetic liposomes delivery of carbon dots nanozymes ameliorate ulcerative colitis by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yana
Zhao, Jingjie
Deng, Zhichao
Gao, Bowen
Xu, Chenxi
Yan, Xiangji
Yang, Mei
Zhang, Yujie
Xu, Qiuran
Zhang, Mingzhen
Xu, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: As a recurrent chronic inflammatory intestinal disease, ulcerative colitis (UC) has seriously affected the life quality of patients. Numerous studies have demonstrated excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) are involved in the occurrence and development of UC, and manipulating ROS expression in the intestinal microenvironment to lower the redox signaling and oxidative stress is a promising strategy in UC therapeutics. We previously demonstrated that carbon-dots (C-dots) nanozymes with superoxide dismutase-like activity could effectively clear ROS and alleviate inflammation, while the accurate lesion location targeting is still a crucial factor in impacting their therapeutic efficiency. In this study, inspired by naturally occurring intercellular interactions, macrophage cell membrane-coated liposomes were constructed to deliver C-dots to amplify their efficiency. The obtained C-dots@Lipo-M endowed C-dots with prolonged circulation time and inflammation targeting capability. In addition, this delivery platform could be co-localized with mitochondria where ROS generated. In dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced UC models, C-dots@Lipo-M exerted satisfactory preventive and therapeutic effects on colitis by reversing the shortened colon length, scavenging ROS, decreasing the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and reducing barrier protein expression at colon sites. Further transcriptome sequencing revealed that C-dots@Lipo-M might alleviate intestinal inflammation by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment. Collectively, this study provides a new targeted strategy to improve nanozymes therapeutic efficiency and extends their biomedical applications on inflammation-related diseases.

We also thank Huimin Tong at the Instrument Analysis Center of Xi’an Jiaotong University for assisting with TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and ROS scavenging properties of C-dots@Lipo-M. (A) Cyro-TEM of C-dots@Lipo. Scale bar: 100 nm. (B) Representative hydrodynamic diameters for Lipo, C-dots@Lipo, and C-dots@Lipo-M. (C) Zeta potentials for C-dots, Lipo, C-dots@Lipo, C-dots@Lipo-M, and Membrane (n = 3). (D) Agarose gel electrophoresis for C-dots, Lipo, and C-dots@Lipo. (E) The total antioxidant capacity of C-dots and C-dots@Lipo (n = 3). (F) The inhibition rate of O2 •− of C-dots and C-dots@Lipo (n = 3). (G) •OH scavenging capability of C-dots and C-dots@Lipo. (H) SDS-PAGE of different samples. 1. Cell lysis; 2. Macrophage membrane; 3. C-dots@Lipo; 4. C-dots@Lipo-M. (I) Western Blotting.

In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of C-dots@Lipo-M. (A) The fluorescence colocalization of C-dots@Lipo-M with mitochondria. Scale bar: 10 μm. (B) PCC, Manders’ colocalization coefficients, and plot profile corresponding to Figure A. (C) The fluorescence images after incubation DCFH-DA with cells in different groups. Scale bar: 100 μm. (D) The quantitative results by flow cytometry to results in Figure C. (E) The mRNA expression level of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-12, and iNOS (n = 3).

Bio-distribution and inflammation targeting of C-dots@Lipo-M. (A) In vivo imaging of C-dots@Lipo-M at the different time points in healthy mice. (B) Ex vivo imaging of C-dots@Lipo-M at different time points. H: Heart; Li: Liver; S: Spleen; L: Lung; K: Kidney; C: Colon. (C) The fluorescence intensity statistics of C-dots@Lipo-M in different organs at different time points refer to Figure B (n = 3). (D) Pharmacokinetic curve of C-dots@Lipo-M in the blood. (E) In vivo imaging of C-dots@Lipo and C-dots@Lipo-M in colitis mice. (F) Ex vivo imaging of C-dots@Lipo and C-dots@Lipo-M in colitis mice. (G) The fluorescence intensity statistics of colon tissues refer to Figure F (n = 3).

The preventive effects of C-dots@Lipo-M on UC. (A) The experimental procedure of C-dots@Lipo-M in the prevention of UC. (B) Representative images of hematochezia in the different groups. (C) The endoscope images of the colons. (D) Body weight changes of mice during the experiment. (E) DAI scores of mice during the experiment. (F) The statistics of colon length. 1. Control, 2. DSS, 3. C-dots + DSS, 4. C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS. (G) The H&E images of the colons. Scale bar: 100 μm. (H) The DHE staining images of the colons. Scale bar: 100 μm. (I-J) The immunofluorescence images of the barrier proteins (ZO-1, Occludin) at colon sites. Scale bar: 100 μm. (K) The mRNA expression level of the pro-inflammatory cytokines at colon sites (n = 3). 1. Control, 2. DSS, 3. C-dots + DSS, 4. C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS.

The therapeutic effects of C-dots@Lipo-M on UC. (A) The experimental procedure of C-dots@Lipo-M on a delayed treatment of UC. (B) The body weight changes of the mice in different groups. (C) The DAI score in different groups. (D) The colon length statistics. 1. Control; 2. DSS; 3. DSS + C-dots; 4. DSS + C-dots@Lipo-M. (E) The H&E images of colons. Scale bar: 100 μm. (F) The DHE staining images of the colons. Scale bar: 100 μm. (G) The immunofluorescence images of barrier proteins ZO-1 at colon sites. Scale bar: 100 μm. (H) The mRNA expression level of pro-inflammatory cytokines at colon sites (n = 3).

The underlying mechanisms exploitation of C-dots@Lipo-M by transcriptome sequencing analysis. (A) Differential gene clustering heatmap of the three sample tissues. (B) Volcano plots of all genes in the DSS group and C-dots@Lipo-M group. The red dots represented the upregulated DEGs, the blue dots were downregulated DEGs, and the grey dots were non-differentially expressed genes. (C) Venn diagram between downregulated genes in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS and upregulated genes in DSS vs Control. (D) GO biological process enrichment analysis of the downregulated DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS. (E) GO cellular component enrichment analysis of the downregulated DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS. (F) KEGG pathway enrichment results of differential genes in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS. (G) Differential gene clustering heatmap of the IL-17 signaling pathway. (H) GSEA enrichment analysis of DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS.

The biocompatibility of C-dots@Lipo-M. (A, B) Cell viability of RAW 264.7 and Colon-26 cells after treatment with different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M. (C) Cell apoptosis of RAW 264.7 cells after treatment with different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M by flow cytometry. (D) The hemolytic toxicity of C-dots@Lipo-M. (E) H&E images of the major organs and colon tissues. Scale bar: 100 μm. (F) The concentrations of liver function parameters, kidney function parameters, and myocardial function parameters.

Schematic illustration of C-dots@Lipo-M construction and their application in UC therapeutics. (A) Synthesis of C-dots@Lipo-M. Firstly, cationic liposomes were constructed using MO, DOTAP, and Pluronic F127 as materials. Then, C-dots@Lipo were obtained through electrostatic binding between cationic liposomes and C-dots nanozymes. Further, to enhance the accumulation of C-dots nanozymes at inflammatory sites, macrophage membranes were coated onto C-dots@Lipo to obtain C-dots@Lipo-M. (B) C-dots@Lipo-M efficiently ameliorates UC by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment. With intravenous administration, C-dots@Lipo-M were delivered to the inflamed colon under the guidance of the macrophage membranes, thus alleviating intestinal inflammation efficiently.

head.ce:title: Macrophage-biomimetic liposomes delivery of carbon dots nanozymes ameliorate ulcerative colitis by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a recurrent chronic inflammatory intestinal disease, ulcerative colitis (UC) has seriously affected the life quality of patients. Numerous studies have demonstrated excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) are involved in the occurrence and development of UC, and manipulating ROS expression in the intestinal microenvironment to lower the redox signaling and oxidative stress is a promising strategy in UC therapeutics. We previously demonstrated that carbon-dots (C-dots) nanozymes with superoxide dismutase-like activity could effectively clear ROS and alleviate inflammation, while the accurate lesion location targeting is still a crucial factor in impacting their therapeutic efficiency. In this study, inspired by naturally occurring intercellular interactions, macrophage cell membrane-coated liposomes were constructed to deliver C-dots to amplify their efficiency. The obtained C-dots@Lipo-M endowed C-dots with prolonged circulation time and inflammation targeting capability. In addition, this delivery platform could be co-localized with mitochondria where ROS generated. In dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced UC models, C-dots@Lipo-M exerted satisfactory preventive and therapeutic effects on colitis by reversing the shortened colon length, scavenging ROS, decreasing the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and reducing barrier protein expression at colon sites. Further transcriptome sequencing revealed that C-dots@Lipo-M might alleviate intestinal inflammation by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment. Collectively, this study provides a new targeted strategy to improve nanozymes therapeutic efficiency and extends their biomedical applications on inflammation-related diseases.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic and idiopathic inflammatory disease involving the mucosa of the colon and rectum, leading to erosion and ulcers of the colon [1,2]. Clinical symptoms including abdominal pain, diarrhea, bloody stool, and extra-intestinal complications seriously disturb the patients. The current clinical prescription drugs cannot effectively control inflammation, and they also manifest adverse effects and tolerance under long-term exposure [3–6]. These limitations would further aggravate the colon inflammation, and enhance the risks of colon resection and colorectal cancer [7]. Therefore, it is vital to exploit efficient and alternative strategies based on the pathophysiology of UC. The etiology of UC is composed of various aspects including genes, intestinal microbes, and immune response [8,9], of which reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a potential causative factor in the occurrence of UC. During the development of UC, ROS is overproduced, which then exacerbates the inflammation of the colon by damaging the DNA, protein, and lipid [10,11]. Thus ROS has become a promising therapeutic target in the treatment of UC [12–15].

Nanozymes, with intrinsic enzymatic activity, have attracted increasing attention in biosensing, disease diagnosis, and therapeutics as they show lower cost, higher catalytic stability, adjustable activity, and ease of modification compared with natural enzymes [16–18]. Currently, nanozymes with different activities including oxidoreductases, hydrolases, polymerases, and isomerases, have been successively exploited. Nanozymes with oxidoreductases such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT) could regulate the ROS level, thus maintaining redox homeostasis, and show great potential in the treatment of inflammatory diseases [19,20]. We previously demonstrated carbon dots (C-dots) with SOD enzymatic activity possessed preferable ROS scavenging capability, and effectively alleviated inflammation, providing a novel strategy for UC treatment [21,22]. However, Nanoparticles with a size < 5 nm are easily excreted out from clearing organs including the liver and kidney especially the kidney as the pore size of the glomerular filtration is about 4.5–5 nm [23,24]. In our previous studies C-dots with small sizes could be quickly metabolized out of the body. Moreover, C-dots without targeting capability would decrease their efficiency and increase the adverse effect.

The development of nanotechnology provides the possibility for the highly efficient delivery of drugs, thus increasing the therapeutic efficiency of drugs. Nanoparticles as delivery carriers could not only prolong the circulation time of drugs but also endow the drugs with active targeting capability [25–27]. To date, various nanocarriers such as polymers, liposomes, and micelles have been widely used in nanomedicine. Among them, liposomes have become promising delivery systems as they show several advantages such as enhancing circulation time, increasing tissue penetration, and ease of modification. In addition, liposomes exhibit a diversity of structures and functions, which can deliver both hydrophobic and hydrophilic molecules [28–29].

Nanoparticles without any functional decorations are hard to exert optimal therapeutic effects, on the one hand, they can be easily captured by the reticuloendothelial system (RES); on the other hand, without any targeting modification, nanoparticles are likely off-target at non-inflammatory sites [30,31]. Cell membrane biomimetic strategy is a promising method for the performance improvement of nanocarriers [32,33]. Coating nanoparticles with cell membranes endows them with biological characteristics of cell membranes such as long circulation time and disease targeting. For example, red blood cells (RBC) own a life span of 120 days and “self” identities such as CD47. Thus RBC membrane-coated nanoparticles exhibit long circulation time and immune evasion ability [34]. Leukocytes, such as monocytes and neutrophils, are tightly associated with inflammatory processes. During the progression of inflammation, leukocytes are recruited to inflamed sites, where they participate in innate immune and adapted immune responses simultaneously. Nanoparticles with leukocyte membranes cloaking could not only avoid phagocytosis and clearance by macrophages but also could target tumors or dysfunctional vascular [35,36].

In this study, we introduced macrophage membrane biomimetic liposomes delivery of C-dots nanozymes for the targeting and highly efficient treatment of ulcerative colitis. As shown in Scheme 1 , cationic liposomes (Lipo) were synthesized through a thin-film hydration method using monoolein (MO), 1, 2-dioleoyl-3-trimethyl-ammonium-propane (DOTAP), and Pluronic F127 as raw materials. Then the negative C-dots bonded to liposomes through electrostatic bonds to form C-dots@Lipo. To enhance the accumulation of C-dots at inflammatory sites, macrophage membranes were coated on C-dots@Lipo to obtain C-dots@Lipo-M. The macrophage membranes endowed C-dots with longer circulation time and inflammation-targeting capability. In the animal models of UC, C-dots@Lipo-M could be delivered to inflammatory sites under the guidance of the macrophage membranes, where they mitigated intestinal inflammation by modulating inflammation pathways and remodeling the redox microenvironment.

MO was purchased from Rhawn Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DOTAP was purchased from Xi’an Ruixi Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Pluronic F127, nitro tetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), L-methionine (L-met), riboflavin, and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Total antioxidant capacity assay kit with ABTS method, ROS assay kit, DHE probe, Hoechst, Mito-Tracker Green, Lyso-Tracker Green, and Actin-Tracker Green were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology. Cy5.5 was purchased from Lumiprobe Corporation. The antibodies anti-integrin α4 and anti-integrin β1 were obtained from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc.; anti-Galetin4 was from Servicebio Science & Technology Co., Ltd.; anti-Claudin-1, anti-Occludin, and anti-ZO-1 were obtained from proteintech Group, Inc.

50 mg/mL of MO and 5 mg/mL of DOTAP stock solutions were obtained by dissolving them in ethanol, Pluronic F127 was added into ultra-pure water to form a 5 mg/mL stock solution. For the synthesis of cationic liposomes (Lipo), MO and DOTAP were mixed with a weight ratio of 10:1, followed by ethanol removal to form a thin lipid film in rotary evaporation for 2 h. Then F127 solution was added to hydration under probe sonication (Branson 550, 20 % Power, 3 s-on and 3 s-off) for 5 min, resulting in liposome solutions.

C-dots were synthesized as we previously reported [21]. The C-dots@Lipo solutions were prepared by mixing liposomes and C-dots with a weight ratio of 20:1 for 30 min followed by sonication. The same procedure was used to synthesize C-dots-Cy5.5@Lipo. For the synthesis of Cy5.5@Lipo, Cy5.5 acetonitrile solution was mixed with MO: DOTAP ethanol solutions, followed by the removal of organic solvents and hydration with F127 solutions.

For the synthesis of C-dots@Lipo-M, C-dots@Lipo (1 mg) was mixed with RAW 264.7 cell fragments (0.5 mg) extracted as previously reported [37], then C-dots@Lipo-M was obtained under ice bath ultrasound (800 W, 3 min).

The morphology of C-dots@Lipo was characterized using a cryo-transmission microscope (Cryo-TEM). The size and zeta potential were measured through Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE. The ROS scavenging capacity was detected through microplate reader BioTEK Gen5 and HITACHI spectrophotometer (F-4700), respectively. The encapsulation of C-dots in liposomes was validated through agarose gel electrophoresis. The cell membrane coating was evaluated through sodium dodecyl sulfonate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and western blotting (WB).

The total anti-oxidation capacity of C-dots and C-dots@Lipo was measured through a commercial total antioxidant capacity assay kit with the ABTS method. In brief, the ABTS was mixed with oxidant at a volume ratio of 1:1, and the mixture was stored at room temperature in the dark for 12–16 h. Then different concentrations of C-dots or C-dots@Lipo-M were added. The absorbance at 734 nm wavelength (A734) was recorded and the total antioxidant capability was represented by the inhibition rate of ABTS+, which was calculated using the following formula: Inhibition rate (%) = (A0-A)/A0 × 100 %, where A0 is the A734 of the control group, and A is of the experiment group.

For evaluation of the O2•− scavenging capacity, different concentrations of C-dots or C-dots@Lipo were added to the solutions including L-met (13 mM), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (0.01 mM), NBT (0.05 mM) and riboflavin (0.02 mM), and the absorbance at 560 nm wavelength was recorded after irradiation under LED lamp for 1 min. The O2 •− scavenging capacity is represented by the inhibition rate of O2 •−, the inhibition rate (%)= (A0-A)/A0 × 100 %, of which A0 is the absorbance at 560 nm of the control group, and A is that of the experiment group.

For the scavenging of •OH, C-dots or C-dots@Lipo were reacted with the mixture including 5 mM of H2O2 and 0.5 mM of terephthalic acid (TA), then the reaction system was irradiated under a UV lamp for 15 min. Then the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction system under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm was measured.

RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were cultured in the DMEM medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum, 100 μg/mL of streptomycin, and 100 U/mL of penicillin at 37℃ in a cell culture incubator with 5 % CO2.

RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate pre-treated with cell slides overnight. Cy5.5@Lipo-M was added for 4 h followed by washing with PBS, and Hoechst was used to stain the cell nucleus for 10 min at 37 ℃. Then the mitochondria probe (Mito-Tracker Green) was incubated with cells for 15 min to stain mitochondria. After sealing the glass slide, the cells were observed under the microscope.

Cellular ROS level was measured by a DCFH-DA probe. Briefly, RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate with 5 × 104 cells per well overnight. C-dots or C-dots@Lipo-M (with the concentration of C-dots as 10 μg/mL) were added for another 2 h, followed by inflammation induction by 50 ng/mL of LPS for 12 h. Then the cells were incubated with the 10 μM of DCFH-DA probe for 30 min, and the fluorescence of DCF was imaged using a fluorescence microscope or quantified through flow cytometry (NovoCyte).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-inflammatory effect of C-dots@Lipo-M
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro anti-inflammatory capability of C-dots@Lipo-M was evaluated by quantifying the mRNA expression level of cytokines using quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR). RAW 264.7 cells were seeded into a 6-well plate with 5 × 105 cells per well for 24 h. Then C-dots or C-dots@Lipo-M (with the concentration of C-dots as 10 μg/mL) were added for another 2 h, followed by treatment with 50 ng/mL of LPS for 12 h. RNA was extracted followed by inverse transcription to form cDNA. The cDNA was further used to amplify using qPCR.

Female C57BL/6 mice (6–8 w, 18–20 g) were purchased from GemPharmatech Co. Ltd., Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, China. All mice were kept in standard conditions including light, temperature, water, and food, and the animal experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee, at Xi’an Jiaotong University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo biocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro biocompatibility was evaluated by MTT and cell apoptosis assays. For MTT, RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were seeded into 96-well plates for 24 h, respectively. Then different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M were added for another 24 h. After incubation, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT was used to react with cells for 4 h, followed by the disposal of supernatant and resuspension of precipitation. Then A490 was recorded for the calculation of the cell viability. For cell apoptosis assay, RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells were seeded into 12-well plates. After 24 h, different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M were incubated with cells for another 24 h. Then cell suspensions were collected, and Annexin V-FITC and PI were added for 15 min in the dark. After adding 1 × Binding Buffer, the fluorescence of FITC and PI were analyzed through flow cytometry.

For the hematotoxicity of C-dots@Lipo-M, fresh blood was collected and red blood cells were separated through centrifugation and dilution. Then PBS (negative control), water (positive control), and different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M were added into red blood cells for 4 h at 37℃. The mixture was centrifuged and the absorbance at 540 nm wavelength of the supernatants was obtained for the calculation of the hemolysis ratio.

For in vivo biocompatibility, healthy C57BL/6 mice were randomly divided into the Control group and the C-dots@Lipo-M group (3 mice in each group). 5 mg/kg of C-dots@Lipo-M was intravenously injected into mice of the C-dots@Lipo-M group every other day three times, and the Control group was administrated with PBS. On day 7, the blood was collected for the blood routine and biochemistry analysis. The colon tissues were used for hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo distribution and targeting capability of C-dots@Lipo-M
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy C57BL/6 mice were administrated with 5 mg/kg of Cy5.5@Lipo-M via the tail vein. After administration for different times (0.5 h, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h), in vivo imaging was conducted through the VISQUE® InVivo Smart-LF system. After in vivo imaging, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) and the colons were collected for ex vivo imaging and further fluorescence statistics. Meanwhile, the blood was obtained for the detection of the fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5, which could be used to calculate the blood half-life of C-dots@Lipo-M.

To assess the inflammation-targeting capability of C-dots@Lipo-M, the UC mice induced by dextran sulfate sodium (DSS) were randomly divided into two groups, one group was injected with Cy5.5@Lipo, and the other group was injected with Cy5.5@Lipo-M. In vivo imaging was performed after 6 h, and then the major organs and the colons were collected for ex vivo imaging and fluorescence statistics. To further observe the fluorescence of Cy5.5 in the colons, frozen sections of colon tissues were performed, and then Actin-Tracker and DAPI were used to stain the cell skeleton and cell nucleus. The images were obtained under a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo prophylactic effect of C-dots@Lipo-M in acute UC
ce:section.ce:para: 24 female C57BL/6 mice were randomly grouped: Control, DSS, C-dots + DSS, C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS. Mice were given consecutively water with 2.5 % of DSS (w/v) for 7 days to establish the UC model, and during the establishment of UC, PBS, C-dots (2 mg/kg), and C-dots@Lipo-M (2 mg/kg) were intravenously administrated into mice, respectively. The conditions such as feces and weight were recorded. Before the end of the experiment, the colons of mice were observed by an endoscope. On day 7, mice were executed, and the colon tissues were collected for H&E staining, ROS measurement, qPCR, and immunostaining [22].
The weight changes, stool, and bloody stool were recorded during the experiment. The DAI was calculated as previously reported [38].

The colon tissues were carried out into frozen sections. After washing with PBS 2 times, ZO-1, Occludin, and Claudin-1 antibodies were incubated with the above-frozen sections following the instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effect of C-dots@Lipo-M in acute UC
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy C57BL/6 mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (n = 6): Control, DSS, DSS + C-dots, DSS + C-dots@Lipo-M. The mice of the Control group were given normal drinking water, while the DSS group mice were given 2.5 % of DSS for 7 d. After 7 d, DSS was changed to normal drinking water, meanwhile, the C-dots group and C-dots@Lipo-M group mice were intravenously administrated with 2 mg/kg C-dots and C-dots@Lipo-M every day for 4 days. The conditions such as feces and weight were recorded. Before the end of the experiment, the colons of mice were observed by an endoscope. On day 10, mice were executed, and the colon tissues were collected for H&E staining, ROS measurement, qPCR, and immunostaining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptome analysis of the colon tissues in acute UC
ce:section.ce:para: C57BL/6 mice were randomly divided into three groups, each with three mice: Control (healthy mice), DSS, and C-dots@Lipo-M. Except for the Control group, mice in the other two groups were given 2.5 % DSS consecutively for 7 days. During the model establishment, 2 mg/kg of C-dots@Lipo-M was injected into mice in the C-dots@Lipo-M group. After 7 days, mice were sacrificed, and the colons were collected for the following experiment. RNA in colon tissues was extracted and purified using TRIzol reagent, the quantification, and purity of RNA were analyzed through NanoDrop ND-1000 (Nanodrop, Wilmington, DE, USA), and the integrity analysis was conducted through Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent, USA). The library construction and sequencing were carried out in LC Bio-Technology CO., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). After sequencing, the raw data was filtered to gain high-quality sequencing data, then they were aligned with the reference genome of mice. The bioinformatics analysis such as KEGG and GO was performed using the OmicStudio tools at https://www.omicstudio.cn/tool.

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests were used to determine the statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001); a p-value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

Firstly, cationic liposomes were synthesized using MO, Pluronic F127, and DOTAP as raw materials. Then, C-dots@Lipo were constructed by electrostatic binding between C-dots nanozymes and cationic liposomes. As previously reported, the C-dots exhibited good dispersion with a size of about 2 nm (Fig. S1A). The morphology characterization of the cationic liposome (Lipo) and C-dots@Lipo by cyro-TEM showed that they were spherical with good dispersion (Fig. S1B, and 1A). The average sizes of Lipo and C-dots@Lipo were 180 nm and 170 nm, respectively by dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Fig. 1 B). For zeta potentials, the values for C-dots nanozymes, Lipo, and C-dots@Lipo were −37 mV, +40 mV, and −7 mV, respectively (Fig. 1C). Agarose gel electrophoresis was then used to evaluate the encapsulation of C-dots nanozymes in Lipo. The results revealed that C-dots nanozymes in C-dots@Lipo were almost retained in the gel hole, suggesting the successful loading of C-dots nanozymes in Lipo (Fig. 1D). We previously confirmed that C-dots nanozymes exhibited the preferable ROS scavenging capacity. To assess whether C-dots@Lipo could eliminate ROS in the same way, total antioxidant capacity, O2•− scavenging capacity, and •OH scavenging capacity of C-dots nanozymes and C-dots@Lipo were examined and compared. As shown in Fig. 1E-F, both C-dots nanozymes and C-dots@Lipo showed similar concentration-dependent inhibition rates for ABTS + and O2•−, suggesting the same enzymatic behavior. While the inhibition rate of C-dots@Lipo was reduced to a certain degree, the superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity for C-dots nanozymes was about 10,000 U/mg, which is much higher than that of natural SOD (∼3500 U/mg) [21,39], we believe both C-dots nanozymes and C-dots@Lipo have distinguished ROS-scavenging capacity. •OH scavenging evaluation showed that C-dots nanozymes and C-dots@Lipo had a similar capacity (Fig. 1G). Collectively, C-dots nanozymes were successfully bonded with Lipo and showed acceptable ROS-scavenging capacity.

Macrophages, as multifunctional leukocytes, are mainly involved in the immune process through phagocytosis, elimination of microorganisms and apoptosis cells, and the presentation of antigens [40,41]. In addition, macrophages exhibit an inflammatory homing effect, the specific membrane protein on them could bind to surface receptors on endothelial cells, thus mediating the inflammation targeting [42]. Accumulating evidence has demonstrated that in the macrophage cell membrane biomimetic nanoparticles, the macrophage membrane could not only protect nanoparticles against RES but also endowed them with the capability of inflammation targeting [43–45]. Therefore, to improve the targeting ability of C-dots@Lipo to inflammatory sites, macrophage membranes were camouflaged onto C-dots@Lipo. With macrophage membrane coating, the size of C-dots@Lipo-M was increased to ∼ 185 nm (Fig. 1B), and the zeta potential was further decreased to −35 mV (Fig. 1C), which was in accord with the negative charge of the cell membrane. To further investigate the membrane coating, SDS-PAGE was conducted to analyze the protein expression profile (Fig. 1H). The protein abundance of the macrophage membrane sample was different from that of cell lysis, which may be due to the removal of proteins in the nucleus and cytoplasm after the extraction. Moreover, the protein expression on C-dots@Lipo-M was similar to that of the membrane sample, demonstrating the retention of macrophage membrane proteins on C-dots@Lipo-M. During inflammation progression, leukocytes such as macrophages were recruited to lesion sites, at which α4β1 integrin on the macrophage surface plays crucial roles involved in inflammation, such as through forming firm adhesion with vascular cell adhesion molecule (VCAM-1) on the endothelium [46,47]. We further examined integrin α4 and integrin β1 on C-dots@Lipo-M. Results showed that when the expression of cell membrane protein galetin4 was consistent in different groups, macrophage membrane markers integrin α4 and integrin β1 in the membrane group and C-dots@Lipo-M group were exposed, which were invisible in the lysis group and C-dots@Lipo group (Fig. 1I). These results demonstrated that C-dots@Lipo-M exhibited the same protein expression with macrophage membrane, suggesting the successful coating of macrophage membranes on C-dots@Lipo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of C-dots@Lipo-M
ce:section.ce:para: Effective internalization and precise subcellular localization of nanoparticles is a prerequisite for their functions [48,49]. To evaluate the internalization of C-dots after loading to liposomes, C-dots-Cy5.5 and C-dots-Cy5.5@Lipo were incubated with RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells for 2 h, respectively. After staining the nucleus with DAPI, the fluorescence images were captured through the fluorescence microscope, and the quantification was conducted through flow cytometry. As shown in Figs. S2A-D, the red fluorescence was stronger in the C-dots-Cy5.5@Lipo group than in the C-dots-Cy5.5 group. These results were further verified by flow cytometry, suggesting the enhanced cellular internalization of C-dots@Lipo. To validate the inflammation targeting capability of macrophage membrane, Cy5.5@Lipo, and Cy5.5@Lipo-M were incubated with inflammatory RAW264.7 cells induced by LPS, then their uptake was evaluated. The results showed that the red fluorescence of Cy5.5 was enhanced after membrane coating compared with the Cy5.5@Lipo group (Fig. S3A). The flow cytometry results were consistent with fluorescence images (Fig. S3B), demonstrating the inflammation targeting of macrophage membrane.

Mitochondria are the primary organelles that produce ROS, if nanoparticles can target mitochondria, the ROS level can be manipulated from the source [50]. In our previous studies, we have demonstrated that C-dots could enter mitochondria [21]. We then evaluated the co-localization of C-dots@Lipo-M with mitochondria. RAW 264.7 cells were incubated with Cy5.5@Lipo-M for 4 h, and Mito-Tracker Green and Hoechst were used to stain mitochondria and cell nuclei (Fig. 2 A-B). The results showed that the red fluorescence of Cy5.5@Lipo-M could merge with the green fluorescence of Mito-Tracker with a Pearson’s colocalization coefficient (PCC) of 0.949, and Manders’ colocalization coefficients of 0.998 and 1, respectively. Moreover, the plot profile also demonstrated the co-location of Cy5.5@Lipo-M with mitochondria. These results indicated that although the exact release mechanism of C-dots from C-dots@Lipo-M was unclear, both the released C-dots and C-dots encapsulated in C-dots@Lipo-M could enter mitochondria, where they would clear the source of ROS, O2 •−, thus cutting down the generation pathways of other ROS, and remodeling the redox microenvironment.

To analyze the in vitro anti-oxidative effect, we measured the cellular ROS level using a DCFH-DA probe. RAW 264.7 cells were pre-treated with C-dots and C-dots@Lipo-M for 2 h, followed by activation with LPS and incubation with DCFH-DA. The microscope results showed that the cells induced by LPS exhibited distinct green fluorescence, indicating ROS production (Fig. 2C). Obvious green fluorescence also appeared in the C-dots nanozymes treatment group, while the fluorescence was reduced and even disappeared after treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M for 2 h. In the quantitative results, the fluorescence intensity of DCF was increased to 60.68 % after LPS induction, which kept almost unchanged after incubation with C-dots nanozymes, while it was reduced to 1.97 % in the C-dots@Lipo-M group (Fig. 2D). In our previous study, we found that the cellular ROS scavenging capability was related to the incubation time of C-dots nanozymes, and incubation C-dots nanozymes for 8 h effectively eliminated ROS, while 2 h exhibited poor effects. However, when C-dots were loaded in liposomes, they showed prominent ROS scavenging capability after incubation for 2 h, demonstrating the enhanced cellular internalization effects of liposomes. Therefore, C-dots@Lipo-M could effectively regulate ROS by facilitating the internalization of C-dots nanozymes.

We also detected the mRNA levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines by qPCR. RAW 264.7 cells were pre-treated with C-dots and C-dots@Lipo-M for 2 h, then activated by LPS. After that, the RNA was extracted and cDNA was obtained for qPCR. As expected, the mRNA expression of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), Interleukin-1β (IL-1β), Interleukin-12 (IL-12), and inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) was increased in the LPS-induced group, which were significantly reduced after treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M (Fig. 2E). Therefore, C-dots@Lipo-M could effectively relieve LPS-induced inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo bio-distribution and inflammation-targeting capability of C-dots@Lipo-M
ce:section.ce:para: To study the bio-distribution of C-dots@Lipo-M, 5 mg/kg of Cy5.5@Lipo-M was intravenously injected into healthy mice, and then in vivo imaging was conducted at different time points. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity in the abdomen gradually increased with the extension of time, and it reached the peak at 3 h, then gradually decreased (Fig. 3 A). The major organs and colons were then collected for ex vivo imaging. As shown in Fig. 3B, C-dots@Lipo-M were mainly accumulated in the liver, lung, and kidney at 30 mins of post-injection. Then the accumulation was decreased in the liver and increased in the kidney. At 12 h, the fluorescence signal was weak in the liver, while the fluorescence reached the maximum in the kidney. For the colon, the fluorescence signal increased gradually and reached a peak value at 6 h, then the signal became weak. Collectively, the trends of fluorescence intensity at major organs and the colons were the same as the imaging results (Fig. 3C). In our previous studies, the size of C-dots nanozymes was below 5 nm, and the in vivo pharmacokinetic results showed that the half-life of C-dots nanozymes was short, thus they could be easily metabolized out of the body quickly [21,22]. This would reduce the accumulation of C-dots nanozymes at the inflammatory sites, which inevitably decline their efficacy. To investigate the metabolism of C-dots@Lipo-M in blood, the fluorescence intensity of Cy5.5 in serum was measured. Fig. 3D was the pharmacokinetic curve of C-dots@Lipo-M, and the half-life was 158 min, which was much longer than C-dots alone. These results demonstrated the biomimetic liposomes could prolong the retention time of C-dots in the body, thus increasing their accumulation at the lesion sites.

To determine the inflammation-targeting capability of the macrophage membranes, we established a DSS-induced UC model. The colitis mice were divided into two groups, one group was administrated with Cy5.5@Lipo, and the other was with Cy5.5@lipo-M. As shown in Fig. 3E, the abdomen fluorescence of mice in the Cy5.5@Lipo-M group was stronger than that of the Cy5.5@Lipo group at 6 h post-injection. The ex vivo results showed that fluorescence intensity has no significant difference in the major organs between the two groups, while the fluorescence intensity in the colon was stronger in the Cy5.5@Lipo-M group (Fig. 3F-G). The fluorescence images exhibited the same tendency (Fig. S4). These results proved that liposomes modified with macrophage membranes exhibited prominent inflammation-targeting capability. Next, we further compared the in vivo distribution of C-dots and C-dots@Lipo-M (Figs. S5A-C). Compared with C-dots-Cy5.5, the intravenously injected C-dots-Cy5.5@Lipo-M exhibited a significantly enhanced fluorescence signal in the colon, demonstrating the inflammation targeting accumulation of C-dots after encapsulation C-dots in biomimetic liposome.

Based on the outstanding anti-oxidative, anti-inflammatory, and inflammation-targeting capability, we are encouraged to study the preventive effect of C-dots@Lipo-M on UC (Fig. 4 A). At the end of the experiment, there was obvious hematochezia in colitis mice, while the hematochezia phenomenon was almost invisible after treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M and C-dots (Fig. 4B). Correspondingly, for endoscopy, the colon structure was intact and the blood vessel texture was clear in the Control group. While in the DSS group, obvious congestion, disappearance of the vascular texture, and ulcers were observed. In the C-dots + DSS and C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS groups, congestion and ulcers disappeared, and the vascular texture was recovered (Fig. 4C). Next, the body weight changes and DAI scores of mice during the experiment were measured. As shown in Fig. 4D, the weight of mice in the DSS group gradually decreased from the 5th day, and it was reduced by 16.2 % on the 7th day, which could be significantly recovered by C-dots@Lipo-M. Similarly, the DAI score was increased after DSS induction, and it reached 2.7 on the 7th day, while administration with C-dots@Lipo-M reversed it to 1 (p < 0.001) (Fig. 4E). Then the collected colons were photographed and the colon length was analyzed. As shown in Fig. S6A and 4F, the colon length in the DSS group (4.4 ± 0.8 cm) was shortened compared with that of the Control group (6.6 ± 0.5 cm), and administration with C-dots@Lipo-M could significantly increase the colon length to 6.1 ± 0.4 cm. The structure and pathological changes of the colons were observed through H&E staining. The results showed that the colons exhibited damaged epithelium, disappeared crypts, decreased or disappeared goblet cells, and obvious inflammatory cell infiltration in colitis mice. While in the C-dots + DSS group especially in the C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS group, the epithelium was recovered, and the inflammatory cells disappeared (Fig. 4G). These results demonstrated that C-dots@Lipo-M could alleviate DSS-induced intestinal inflammation.

During the progression of UC, ROS was overproduced, the intestinal epithelial barrier was damaged, and the pro-inflammatory cytokines were upregulated. Therefore, we compared the ROS level, the mRNA expression level of cytokines, and the epithelial barrier protein expression at colon sites in the different groups. The ROS level was detected through a DHE probe. There was almost no red fluorescence in the healthy group, while apparent red fluorescence was observed in the DSS group (Fig. 4H and S6B). After treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M, the red fluorescence was reduced and even disappeared, indicating that DSS could induce ROS production, and treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M could effectively eliminate ROS.

The intestinal epithelial barrier is responsible for the selective penetration of water and electrolytes through a series of intercellular connections consisting of tight junctions and adherent junctions in normal conditions. In IBD, tight junction-related proteins such as zonula occludins (ZO) and occludin were downregulated, thus the intestinal permeability was changed, causing the invasion of the pathogenic bacteria, and aggravating the inflammation [51–53]. To investigate the changes in the epithelial barrier, we measured the expression of ZO-1, Occludin, and Claudin-1 through immunostaining. As shown in Fig. 4I-J, and S6C-F, the expression of these proteins was all decreased in the DSS group, which was reversed after administration with C-dots@Lipo-M, demonstrating that this biomimetic system could recover the intestinal epithelial barrier.

For cytokines, the mRNA expression of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 was increased after DSS induction, and they were significantly decreased in the C-dots@Lipo-M + DSS group (Fig. 4K), proving that the C-dots@Lipo-M could regulate the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, thus involving the relief of intestinal inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots@Lipo-M effectively alleviated the UC in a delayed treatment
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the therapeutic effect of C-dots@Lipo-M, we established a UC model with delayed treatment of C-dots@Lipo-M (Fig. 5 A). During the experiment, the mice weight in the DSS group was decreased from the fifth day, and extended to the end of the experiment. Meanwhile, the DAI score gradually increased in the DSS group. After treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M, the weight was significantly increased and the DAI score was significantly decreased (Fig. 5B-C). At the end of the experiment, the endoscope was conducted to evaluate the colon changes before the sacrifice of mice. Compared with healthy mice, colons in the DSS group exhibited obvious lesions, and the vascular texture disappeared, while these symptoms were significantly alleviated in the DSS + C-dots@Lipo-M group (Fig. S7A). Then the colon tissues were collected, their length was measured and their pathological changes were investigated. The colon length in the DSS group (4.7 ± 0.2 cm) was shortened compared with healthy mice (6.3 ± 0.4 cm). While administration with C-dots@Lipo-M could recover the length to 5.8 ± 0.3 cm (p < 0.001) (Fig. 5D, and S7B). As for pathological changes of the colons, the epithelium was damaged with the disappearance of crypts and goblet cells and inflammatory cells were infiltrated in the DSS group. While in the DSS + C-dots group especially in the DSS + C-dots@Lipo-M group, crypts were recovered, goblet cells were increased, and inflammatory cells were reduced (Fig. 5E). Moreover, C-dots@Lipo-M could scavenge ROS and increase the expression of tight junction proteins such as ZO-1, Occludin, and Claudin-1 at the colon sites (Fig. 5F-G, and S8A-E). For pro-inflammatory factors, C-dots@Lipo-M could significantly decrease the mRNA expression level of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-12 induced by DSS (Fig. 5H). In general, C-dots@Lipo-M could alleviate intestinal inflammation by scavenging ROS, downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines, and recovering the epithelium barrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The underlying mechanisms of C-dots@Lipo-M on UC therapy
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the underlying mechanisms of C-dots@Lipo-M on UC therapy, the colon tissues in healthy mice, colitis mice, and C-dots@Lipo-M-treated mice were carried out for transcriptome sequencing analysis. Fold change (FC)>=2 and q < 0.05 (i.e. |log2FC|>=1) & q < 0.05) was the threshold standard to screen differentially expressed genes (DEGs) for the following bioinformatics analysis.

Differential gene clustering heatmap could directly display gene expression of the different samples. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the gene profile in the DSS group was significantly different from that of the Control group, while treatment with C-dots@Lipo-M could recover these changes. The Venn diagram results of DEGs in three groups also showed the differential gene expression (Fig. S9A). To analyze the overall distribution of DEGs, all genes in the DSS group and C-dots@Lipo-M group were used for volcano plots (Fig. 6B). The results indicated that most of the DEGs in the C-dots@Lipo-M group were downregulated compared with those of the DSS group. We then analyzed the upregulated genes in DSS vs Control and the downregulated genes in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS. As shown in Fig. 6C, 670 genes were overexpressed in DSS vs Control, of which 475 genes could be downregulated by C-dots@Lipo-M. Similarly, in 245 downregulated genes in DSS vs Control, 38 genes were recovered by C-dots@Lipo-M (Fig. S9B). These results demonstrated that C-dots@Lipo-M could mitigate the inflammation mainly by recovering the up-regulated pro-inflammatory genes induced by DSS.

Gene ontology (GO), as an international classification system of gene function, is consisted of molecular function (MC), cellular component (CC), and biological process (BP). To explore the functions of C-dots@Lipo-M in the treatment of UC, the downregulated DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS were used for GO CC and BP enrichment, respectively (Fig. 6D-E). In the bubble diagram of GO results, the rich factor in the horizontal axis represented the ratio of DEGs in total genes, the bubble size represented the gene number, and the bubble color was the p-value in enrichment analysis. Among the top 20 biological processes, most of the processes are related to inflammatory response processes such as “Cell adhesion”, “Inflammatory response”, “Neutrophil chemotaxis”, “Chemotaxis”, “Wound healing”, and “Chemokine-mediated signaling pathway”. In the results of cellular component analysis, several components of the membrane were enriched, demonstrating the participation of the membrane in inflammation alleviation.

KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes) is a pathway database relevant to gene function. To investigate the related pathways involved in the C-dots@Lipo-M treatment, DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS were conducted for KEGG analysis. As shown in Fig. 6F, pathways including “Rheumatoid arthritis”, “Chemokine signaling pathway”, “IL-17 signaling pathway”, “Phagosome”, “cGMP-PKG signaling pathway”, “Cell adhesion molecules”, “Focal adhesion”, “PI3K-Akt signaling pathway”, “Complement and coagulation cascades”, “Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction” were relevant to inflammation. As the IL-17 signaling pathway plays a crucial role in IBD [54–56], the 22 enriched DEGs in the IL-17 signaling pathway were conducted for heatmap analysis. The results indicated that DSS could change their expression profile (Fig. 6G), while C-dots@Lipo-M could recover them to the same level as in the Control group. These results indicated that C-dots@Lipo-M were involved in the various processes of inflammation.

Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) could be used to exploit effective information of tiny effect genes, so the DEGs in C-dots@Lipo-M vs DSS were analyzed through GO enrichment based on GSEA (Fig. 6H). In the top 30 gene set, genes relevant to mitochondrial ribosomal subunits, mitochondrial assembly, and mitochondrial translation were enriched, implying that C-dots@Lipo-M might mitigate inflammation by regulating the ROS level through the mitochondrial respiration chain.

The biocompatibility of C-dots@Lipo-M was evaluated from in vitro level, blood level, and in vivo level, respectively. The in vitro biosafety was analyzed through MTT and cell apoptosis assay. As shown in Fig. 7 A-B, after incubation different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M (0–100 μg/mL) with RAW 264.7 cells and Colon-26 cells, the cell viability of two cell lines was all above 80 %. In the apoptosis assay, RAW 264.7 cell was exposed to different concentrations of C-dots@Lipo-M for 24 h, then Annexin V-FITC and PI were used to stain the cell. The apoptosis ratio was about 3 % in all groups (Fig. 7C). Similarly, the apoptosis rate in Colon-26 cells was almost unchanged (Fig. S10). These results showed that C-dots@Lipo-M with 0–100 μg/mL exhibited good cellular biocompatibility.

The hemolytic toxicity of nanoparticles would limit their application, thus the blood safety of C-dots@Lipo-M was examined (Fig. 7D). 0–100 μg/mL C-dots@Lipo-M was added to red blood cells for 4 h at 37 ℃. The hemolytic ratio was less than 10 %, demonstrating that C-dots@Lipo-M could be used for intravenous injection.

In vivo biocompatibility was conducted through intravenous administration into mice with 5 mg/kg of C-dots@Lipo-M and saline (in the C-dots@Lipo-M and Control group). During the experiment, the body weight changes of mice in the Control group and C-dots@Lipo-M group showed no significant difference (Fig. S11A). After 7 d, the blood samples, major organ tissues, and colon tissues were collected for further analysis. As shown in Fig. S11B, the blood routine parameters such as white blood cell (WBC) and red blood cell (RBC) both in the two groups were all in the normal range, and they exhibited no significance, indicating the C-dots@Lipo-M showed no systematic toxicity. Then H&E staining was carried out to analyze the structural and pathological changes in the major organs and colons, and the results showed no apparent difference (Fig. 7E and S11C). In addition, C-dots@Lipo-M did not change the functional parameters of the liver, kidney, and heart (Fig. 7F). All demonstrated that C-dots@Lipo-M exhibited preferable biocompatibility.

In summary, we constructed a macrophage membrane biomimetic liposome delivery system of C-dots SOD nanozymes for UC therapeutics. The as-prepared C-dots@Lipo-M enabled C-dots with inflammation-targeting capability and increased residence time simultaneously. At the cellular level, C-dots@Lipo-M could co-localize with mitochondria, scavenge the ROS, and reduce the cytokine expression. In colitis models, intravenous administration with C-dots@Lipo-M could effectively prevent and alleviate DSS-induced inflammation through scavenging ROS, decreasing cytokines excretion, and reducing epithelial barrier protein expression. The transcriptome sequencing revealed that C-dots@Lipo-M were involved in the modulation of inflammatory processes and mitochondrial functions. Collectively, this study provides a new targeted strategy to improve nanozymes therapeutic efficiency and extends their biomedical applications on inflammation-related diseases.

Y.N.M, J.J.Z, Q.R.X, C.L.X, and M.Z.Z designed and conducted the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Z.C.D, B.W.G, and C.X.X participated in grammar revision. X.J.Y and M.Y gave a direct guide for animal experiments. Y.J.Z guided the data analysis. M.Z.Z supervised and supported this study and critically revised the manuscript.
